skill.name: recursive-knowledge
skill.description: Build a local knowledge graph from large document collections, then answer multi-hop questions with traversal state, confidence scoring, and provenance.
skill.summary: Turn large document corpora into a queryable knowledge graph for multi-hop research questions.
skill.author: Cornjebus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are mostly markdown backticks, examples, and SHA-256 identifiers rather than command execution or password storage. The skill does include executable Python scripts that read local corpora, write graph files, and can send document chunks to the Anthropic API when USE_LLM=1 is set, so publication should include a privacy and filesystem warning. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Optional LLM API Transmission of Corpus Text
medium_findings.description: When USE_LLM=1 is set, the indexer creates an Anthropic client and sends extraction prompts containing document chunks. This appears legitimate and opt-in, but sensitive corpora could leave the local environment if users enable it without understanding the data flow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly gates an Anthropic API call behind USE_LLM=1 and passes prompts built from document chunks. The behavior is documented and opt-in, so the concern is privacy exposure rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Local File Reads and Graph Writes
medium_findings.description: The CLI accepts input and output paths, recursively reads .txt, .md, and .json files, and writes a graph file. This is expected for a corpus indexer, but it can process sensitive local files or overwrite outputs if invoked with broad paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access is direct and controlled by CLI paths, which confirms the behavior. It is also central to the skill purpose, so the risk is operational misuse rather than suspicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown code fences, inline identifiers, table values, and CLI examples. The Python scripts do not invoke shell commands or spawn subprocesses.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed the flagged lines are documentation formatting or examples. A targeted search found no subprocess, os.system, popen, eval, or exec use in the scripts.

low_findings.title: SHA-256 Usage Is for Deterministic Identifiers
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections map to SHA-256 content hashes and deterministic graph IDs. They are not used for password storage, signatures, encryption, or authentication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed hash operations create short IDs and document change hashes. There is no security-sensitive cryptographic use in the cited code.

low_findings.title: Graph Output Can Reveal Source Paths and Metadata
low_findings.description: The generated graph stores document paths, titles, content hashes, chunk counts, and source document references. This is useful provenance data, but users should avoid sharing graph files from sensitive corpora without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly serializes document metadata into the graph output. The information is metadata rather than raw secrets, so severity remains low.

dangerous_patterns.title: Opt-In External LLM Call
dangerous_patterns.description: The script can call the Anthropic API when USE_LLM=1 is present, sending prompts that include document text for extraction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external call is explicit in the code and is gated by an environment variable. It is legitimate functionality but requires user awareness for private documents.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local Corpus Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The indexer recursively selects supported document files from a user-supplied directory and writes the resulting graph to a user-supplied output path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is visible and required for the skill. It is a publishable risk when documented, not evidence of malicious intent.

content.user_title: Build Queryable Knowledge Graphs from Large Corpora
content.value_statement: Large document sets can exceed model context limits and make cross-document reasoning unreliable. This skill builds a local graph and queries it with stateful traversal, confidence scoring, and provenance.
actual_capabilities: Indexes .txt, .md, and .json files into a local knowledge graph.
Extracts entities and relationships with either heuristics or an optional Claude API call.
Stores document provenance, content hashes, source references, and graph metadata.
Queries the graph with visited-node and visited-edge tracking to avoid repeated traversal.
Scores answers using confidence, corroborating source counts, and traversal limits.
Provides reference material for graph schema, state management, and traversal patterns.
limitations: The default heuristic extraction is basic and may miss complex entities or relationships.
Optional Claude API extraction can transmit document text outside the local environment.
The query engine returns simple synthesized answers rather than full natural language reports.
It does not include access control, redaction, or encryption for generated graph files.

use_cases.title: Map Research Collections
use_cases.description: Create a graph from papers, notes, and source files so related entities and claims can be traced across documents.

use_cases.title: Analyze Internal Knowledge Bases
use_cases.description: Index technical documentation and project notes to answer questions that depend on several linked documents.

use_cases.title: Prepare Evidence-Backed Briefs
use_cases.description: Use graph provenance to identify supporting sources, contradictions, and important entities before writing a report.

prompt_templates.title: Index a Folder
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a local document collection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use recursive-knowledge to index my local document folder into a graph file. Keep the run local and summarize the graph statistics after indexing.

prompt_templates.title: Ask a Multi-Hop Question
prompt_templates.scenario: Query a graph when the answer may span several documents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use recursive-knowledge to answer this question against my existing graph. Show the answer, confidence, source count, and the key path used.

prompt_templates.title: Find Conflicting Claims
prompt_templates.prompt: Use recursive-knowledge traversal patterns to look for claims that support or contradict this topic. Report the strongest evidence and source references.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Traversal Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis with stricter evidence requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use recursive-knowledge with a deeper traversal limit and higher confidence threshold. Explain how the stopping conditions affected the final answer.

output_examples.input: Question: Who worked with Alice on Project Orion across the indexed documents?
output_examples.output: The answer identifies collaborators connected to both Alice and Project Orion, reports confidence, lists source document counts, and explains the intersecting graph path.

output_examples.input: Question: Are there conflicting claims about the launch date?
output_examples.output: The result separates supporting and contradicting evidence, names the related entities, and highlights which documents contributed each claim.

output_examples.input: Task: Index a folder of project notes and summarize the graph.
output_examples.output: The response reports document count, entity count, relationship count, top entity types, and whether any files were skipped as already indexed.

best_practices: Index only the folders needed for the task, especially when documents may contain sensitive information.
Review graph files before sharing them because they include source paths and provenance metadata.
Use higher confidence and source thresholds for decisions that require strong evidence.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for small document sets that already fit in context.
Do not enable USE_LLM=1 for confidential corpora without approval for external API processing.
Do not treat heuristic extraction results as complete without reviewing important entities and relationships.

faq.question: Does this skill run external shell commands?
faq.answer: No shell command execution was found in the scripts. The documentation shows command examples for users to run manually.

faq.question: Does indexing stay local by default?
faq.answer: Yes. The default path uses heuristic extraction and local file processing only.

faq.question: When does it contact an external API?
faq.answer: It can call the Anthropic API only when USE_LLM=1 is set before indexing.

faq.question: What file types can it index?
faq.answer: It indexes .txt, .md, and .json files from a file path or recursive folder scan.

faq.question: What does the graph output contain?
faq.answer: It contains entities, relationships, document metadata, source document links, content hashes, and provenance fields.

faq.question: Is it suitable for real-time data?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for static corpora and incremental updates, not real-time streams.
